claims.claim: Overcharging causes a decrease in the Lower Explosion Limit (LEL) of thermal runaway gas of Li-ion batteries, resulting in increased danger.
claims.quote: Overcharging causes a decrease in the LEL of thermal runaway gas, and there is a significant difference in the LEL variation of thermal runaway gas under different charge and discharge temperature environments (CTE).

claims.claim: At a charge/discharge ambient temperature of 40°C, overcharging decreases the LEL of thermal runaway gas by 0.32%, which is greater than the 0.19% decrease observed at 30°C.
claims.quote: In the charge/discharge ambient temperature of 30°C, the LEL decreases by 0.19% with the increase of the cutoff voltage, while in the charge/discharge environment of 40°C, the overcharge decreases the LEL by 0.32%.

claims.claim: The LEL of thermal runaway gas is lowest for batteries charged and discharged at 40°C, and in the range of 40–60°C, LEL increases with increasing temperature.
claims.quote: In the charge/discharge ambient temperature of 40–60°C, the LEL increases with the increase of temperature, and the change of LEL is relatively small and the pattern is not obvious at 50–60°C. The LEL of the battery charged and discharged in the ambient temperature of 40°C is the lowest.

claims.claim: The decrease in LEL of thermal runaway gas mixture is caused by a decrease in CO2 content and an increase in vaporized DMC content in the gas.
claims.quote: The decrease of thermal runaway gas LEL is due to the decrease of CO2 content in the gas and the increase of vaporized Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DMC) content.

claims.claim: At 40°C CTE, the percentage of gaseous DMC among thermal runaway gases is 4% higher than at 30°C for normally charged batteries (4.2 V), and 20% higher at a cutoff voltage of 4.4 V.
claims.quote: the percentage of gaseous DMC among the thermal runaway gases of the Li-ion battery at 40°C CTE is 4% higher than that at 30°C, as shown in Fig. 4 (4.2 V). The gas increment is more pronounced under overcharge conditions, with 20% higher at a cutoff voltage of 4.4 V.

claims.claim: The exothermic enthalpy (ΔH) of the anode material mixture reaction decreases with increasing cut-off voltage: 25.35 J/g at 4.2 V, 15.13 J/g at 4.3 V, and 10.11 J/g at 4.4 V, indicating less electrolyte reacts with anode material as overcharge degree increases.
claims.quote: The ΔH of exotherm of the Li-ion battery anode material mixture with a cutoff voltage of 4.2 V is 25.34802 J/g, ΔH of 4.3 V is 15.12939 J/g, and ΔH of 4.4 V is 10.11289 J/g. The amount of exotherm decreases with increasing cutoff voltage, indicating that less electrolyte reacts with the anode material as the degree of overcharge increases.

claims.claim: The overcharge cycle leads to thickening of the SEI membrane, causing incomplete reaction to produce CO2 during thermal runaway, resulting in decreased CO2 content and increased CO content in the gas mixture.
claims.quote: The overcharge cycle leads to the thickening of the SEI membrane, and the heat does not react completely to produce CO2 in the thermal runaway stage. The decrease in CO2 content and the increase in CO content may come from the incomplete reaction of sub-stable substances and the reaction between electrolyte and lithium salt.

claims.claim: The LEL of CO is 12%, which is significantly higher than that of other combustible hydrocarbons (1.5%–5%).
claims.quote: the LEL of CO is 12%, which is significantly higher than that of other combustible hydrocarbons (1.5%–5%)

claims.claim: At temperatures above 50°C CTE, the content of vaporized DMC in thermal runaway gas decreases compared to 40°C, because high-temperature improves cathode material activity and promotes earlier reaction between DMC and the negative electrode material.
claims.quote: increasing the CTE can improve the activity of the materials inside the Li-ion battery and inhibit lithium precipitation, which may promote the reaction between the cathode material and DMC, thus reducing the content of vaporized DMC in the thermal runaway gas... the content of DMC in the thermal runaway gas of the battery at 50°C as well as 60°C CTE is lower than that at 40°C, indicating that the effect of cathode material activity on DMC is higher than that of internal gas production when the temperature is above 50°C.

claims.claim: The negative electrode material of a lithium battery in normal use (4.2 V) only undergoes melting reaction at 700°C or above, but overcharging to 4.4 V causes an additional weight loss step at 96°C and 665°C, indicating decreased thermal stability of the negative electrode material.
claims.quote: the negative electrode material of the lithium battery in normal use only undergoes melting reaction at 700°C or above, and overcharging decreases the thermal stability of the negative electrode material... the negative electrode materials of Li-ion batteries with cutoff voltages of 4.4 V experience two weight loss steps at 96°C and 665°C.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of thermal runaway (T_onset) monotonically increases from 353 K to 417 K as SOC decreases from 120% to 30% for NCM cells.
claims.quote: T_onset monotonically increases from 353 K to 417 K with SOC decreasing from 120% to 30%

claims.claim: The maximum temperature (T_max) during thermal runaway decreases with decreasing SOC for NCM cells, indicating less reactive exothermic reactions at lower SOC.
claims.quote: T_max is decreased with decreased SOC, indicating the corresponding cell is less reactive with suppressed exothermic reactions

claims.claim: For NCM cells with SOC of 120%, the cell temperature rapidly increases from approximately 450 K to approximately 700 K with a maximum temperature rise rate (dT/dt)_max of order 10^4 K/min during Stage III.
claims.quote: Taking the SOC 120% cell as an example, the cell temperature rapidly increases from ∼ 450 K to ∼ 700 K with (𝑑𝑇 ∕𝑑𝑡)𝑚𝑎𝑥 of (104) K⋅min−1

claims.claim: For NCM cells with SOC of 30%, the maximum temperature rise rate (dT/dt)_max decreases to order 10^2 K/min with T_max of approximately 600 K during Stage III.
claims.quote: for the SOC 30% cell, (𝑑𝑇 ∕𝑑𝑡)𝑚𝑎𝑥 decreases to (102) K⋅min−1 with 𝑇𝑚𝑎𝑥 of ∼ 600 K

claims.claim: As SOC increases from 50% to 120%, the total mass loss of NCM cells after thermal runaway increases by approximately a factor of two.
claims.quote: As SOC increases from 50% to 120%, the total mass loss increases by approximately a factor of two, indicating a more complete reactive material decomposition within the cell

claims.claim: The NCM811 prismatic cell (62 Ah) discharged 4.83 mol of combustible gas during thermal runaway, with gas generation rate of 0.0779 mol/Ah.
claims.quote: By calculating the difference in gas volume before and after the experiment, the amount of gas is determined to be 4.83 mol. The gas generation of this battery is 0.0779 mol/Ah.

claims.claim: The main venting gas components of the NCM811 battery during thermal runaway are CO2 (28.6%), CO (27.9%), H2 (20.5%), C2H4 (12.5%), and CH4 (5.5%), with approximately 68.1% of the venting gas being flammable.
claims.quote: the main components of the venting gas of NCM811 battery in the order of high to low are: CO2, CO, H2, C2H4, CH4, O2. About 68.1 % of the venting gas is flammable, which has potential combustion risk.

claims.claim: The area 1 m above the safety valve exhibits the highest combustion risk and greatest explosive power for flammable gas vented from the NCM811 battery.
claims.quote: The high-risk area is 1 m above the safety valve. Experimental results indicate that explosions occur in locations distant from the safety valve. At 5.97 s and 8.58 s, fireballs appeared near the location 1 m above the safety valve.

claims.claim: Near the safety valve outlet, the combustible gas concentration exceeds the upper explosive limit (UEL), resulting in a low combustion risk index of zero at that location; the highest combustion risk is located farther from the valve.
claims.quote: At the battery safety valve, the flammable gas concentration exceeds the UEL with a risk index of 0. Differential diffusion of gas concentrations on either side of the safety valve creates an ignition risk zone. Far from the safety valve, the flammable gas concentration falls within the explosive range, forming a large risk area.

claims.claim: As jet velocity of the battery decreases, the risk of combustible gas explosion and combustion at the safety valve opening increases, with the highest risk period occurring during the initial and final stages of the jet.
claims.quote: As the jet velocity of the battery decreases the risk of combustible gas explosion and combustion at the safety valve opening increases. The period with the highest risk of jet-induced explosion and combustion occurs during the initial and final stages of the jet.
